Contributions.pageTitle: 401(k) contribution information (per pay period)

deductions.pageTitle:
Tax deductions
Enter information for any item or expenditure subtracted from gross income to reduce your taxable income.

labels.title: How do 401(k) salary deductions affect my take-home pay?
labels.answer: Your take-home pay each pay period is %result%

taxexemptdeduction.title: Deduction exempt from the following taxes
taxexemptdeduction.definition: For each deduction that you enter (other than for contributions to a 401(k) plan), enter the types of taxes from which the deduction is exempt. The four types include Social Security, Medicare, state income, and federal income taxes.

employer401kcontributionpayperiod.title: Your employer's 401(k) plan contribution per pay period
employer401kcontributionpayperiod.definition: Enter the amount or percentage that your employer contributes each pay period to your 401(k) plan. Employers often make matching contributions to your plan. A partially matching contribution occurs when your employer contributes an amount less than $1 for each $1 of your contributions. A fully matching contribution is when your employer contributes $1 for each $1 of your contributions. For 2025, the most you may contribute to a 401(k) plan is $23,500. If you are age 50 or older, you may contribute up to $31,000. Total contributions, including those of your employer, may not exceed the lesser of $70,000 ($76,500 if age 50 or older) or 100% of your annual compensation.

matchingcontribution.title: Matching contribution
matchingcontribution.definition: A matching contribution is one where the plan sponsor, usually the employer, matches employee contributions to a 401(k) or other qualified retirement plan.

dcplan401k.title: 401(k) plan
dcplan401k.definition: A 401(k) plan is a defined-contribution plan that permits employees to have a portion of their salary deducted from their paycheck and contributed to an account. Federal (and usually state) taxes on the employee contributions and investment earnings are deferred until the participant receives a distribution from the plan (typically at retirement). Employers may also contribute to the account of a participant. For 2025, you may contribute up to $23,500 to a 401(k) plan. If you are age 50 or older, you may contribute an additional $7,500, or a total of $31,000. If your employer makes contributions, the most that may be contributed to a 401(k) plan for 2025 is the lesser of $70,000 ($76,500 if age 50 or older) or 100% of your annual compensation.

catchupprovisiondcplans.title: Catch-up provision for contributions to retirement plans
catchupprovisiondcplans.definition: A provision of The Economic Growth and Tax Relief Reconciliation Act of 2001 that authorizes higher yearly contribution limits to IRAs, 401(k) plans, and other defined-contribution retirement plans for workers who are age 50 or older.

amountwithheldbyirs.title: Amount withheld by IRS
amountwithheldbyirs.definition: The amount of your income that is withheld each pay period for payment of federal income taxes. Your employer sends this amount to the IRS on your behalf.

yearly401kcontributionlimit.title: Yearly 401(k) plan contribution limit
yearly401kcontributionlimit.definition: For 2025, the most you may contribute to a 403(b) plan is $23,500. If you are age 50 or older, you may contribute up to $31,000. Total contributions, including those of your employer, may not exceed the lesser of $70,000 ($76,500 if age 50 or older) or 100% of your annual compensation.

stateincometaxes.title: State income taxes
stateincometaxes.definition: The taxes you pay, if applicable, to the treasury or revenue board of the state of your legal domicile.

dcplan403b.title: 403(b) plan
dcplan403b.definition: A defined-contribution plan offered to employees of universities and non-profit organizations that lets you make tax-deferred contributions to your own retirement account.

grossincome.title: Gross income
grossincome.definition: The IRS defines gross income as all income that is not exempt from tax. Gross income may be received as money, goods, property, or services.

payperiodfrequency.title: Pay period frequency
payperiodfrequency.definition: Select the appropriate pay-period frequency from the drop-down menu. Choices include daily, weekly, bi-weekly (26 periods in a year), semi-monthly (twice a month), monthly, quarterly, semi-yearly (twice a year), and yearly. Most pay periods are bi-weekly, semi-monthly, or monthly.

totalexemptions.title: Total exemptions
totalexemptions.definition: Either the number of exemptions from your individual income tax return (IRS 1040) or the number of allowances from your withholding allowance certificate (W-4 form).

deduction401k.title: 401(k) deduction
deduction401k.definition: You are allowed to contribute up to $23,500 to a 401(k) plan for 2025 ($31,000 if age 50 or older). The amount of contributions you make is deducted from your gross income. As a result, your taxable income and amount of taxes you owe are lower.

contributions.title: contribution
contributions.definition: The dollar amount you to contribute to tax-advantaged retirement account. The most you can contribute in 2025 for regular and Roth IRAs is $7,000 ($8,000 for those aged 50 or higher). For 401(k) plans, the most you can contribute in 2025 is $23,500 ($31,000 for those aged 50 or higher). Your employer is allowed to provide matching contributions up to a certain amount each year.

grossincomeytd.title: Gross income (year-to-date)
grossincomeytd.definition: Gross income (year-to-date) is your total income through the most recent pay period for the current calendar year.

yourtaxfilingstatus.title: Your tax filing status
yourtaxfilingstatus.definition: While the IRS has five tax-filing statuses, you will see that a tax filing status called "Supplemental" has been automatically selected. This is used to identify the bonus as supplemental income, which the calculator assumes to be taxed at a flat rate of 28%.

additionalwithholdings.title: Additional income withheld by the IRS
additionalwithholdings.definition: Enter any additional amounts that you have requested the IRS withhold from your pay.

payperioddeduction.title: Amount per pay period for this tax deduction
payperioddeduction.definition: Payroll deduction is an automated process that deducts pre-set amounts from the gross pay of employees for making employee contributions to retirement plans and cafeteria plans.

section457plan.title: Section 457 plan
section457plan.definition: A retirement plan for employees of state and local governments that is used instead of a 401(k) or 403(b) plan. For 2025, you may contribute up to $23,500 to a 457 plan (or up to $31,000 if you are age 50 or older).

dcplan.title: Defined-contribution retirement plan
dcplan.definition: An employer-sponsored plan in which contributions are made to individual participant accounts and the final benefit consists solely of assets (including investment returns) that have accumulated in these individual accounts.

taxfilingstatus.title: Tax filing status
taxfilingstatus.definition: A category closely tied to marital status that defines the type of tax return form an individual will use.

medicaretaxes.title: Medicare taxes
medicaretaxes.definition: Medicare taxes are paid to the Medicare (HI) fund.

socialsecuritytaxes.title: Social Security taxes
socialsecuritytaxes.definition: Social Security taxes are paid to Old Age Survivors and Disability Insurance (OASDI) and Medicare.

your401kcontributionpayperiod.title: Your 401(k) plan contribution per pay period
your401kcontributionpayperiod.definition: Enter the amount or percentage that you contribute each pay period to your 403(b) plan. Employers generally let you choose to contribute a flat amount or percentage of salary. For 2025, the most you may contribute to a 403(b) plan is $23,500. If you are age 50 or older, you may contribute up to $31,000. Total contributions, including those of your employer, may not exceed the lesser of $70,000 ($76,500 if age 50 or older) or 100% of your annual compensation.

deduction.title: Deduction
deduction.definition: An amount that you subtract from your taxable income. As a result, a deduction lowers your taxable income, which, in turn, lowers your tax liability.

deductionsfrompay.title: Deductions from pay
deductionsfrompay.definition: The total amount of deductions taken from each paycheck, including your 401(k) plan contributions.

netincome.title: Net income
netincome.definition: Individuals: Net income is the amount of income left after all deductions and taxes. Corporations: Net income is a company's profit for a given period of time after it pays taxes and all other expenses.

help.textLabel: This tool calculates your take-home pay after deducting income taxes and contributions to a 401(k) or similar retirement plan that is sponsored by your employer. Similar retirement plans include 403(b) and Section 457 plans.
Because contributions to a 401(k) or other qualified retirement plan are deducted from gross income, you reduce the amount of income on which you owe taxes.
This tool also adds any matching contributions that your employer makes to your retirement plan.

chartTitles.title: Pie chart of the breakdown of gross income per pay period by federal income taxes,state income taxes, Social Security taxes, Medicare taxes, deductions and net income.

tableTitles.title: Breakdown of gross income per pay period by federal income taxes,state income taxes, Social Security taxes, Medicare taxes, deductions and net income
